speaker: interviewer
text: December 19, 1995. Survivor's name is Marika Somerstein. The maiden name is Schonfeld. The interviewer is Herbert Pasternak. This interview is taking place in the town of Lawrence in New York state in the United States of America, and the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is December 19, 1995. The survivor we will be interviewing today is Marika Somerstein. Her maiden name was Schonfeld. I am the interviewer. My name is Herbert Pasternak. We are in the town of Lawrence in the state of New York in the United States of America. This interview will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask for your last name, please, and may I ask you to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somerstein, S-O-M-E-R-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name, please? May I ask you to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schonfeld, S-C-H-O-N-F-E-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your first name, please, and may I ask you to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marika, M-A-R-I-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a middle name. I no longer use it. It's Agnes, A-G-N-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marika is a nickname.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is the actual name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marianna-- M-A-R-I-A-N-N-A, but no one would know me by that name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: What town were you born in? May I ask you to spell it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nyiregyhaza-- N-Y-I-R-E-G-Y-A-Z-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask you about that town of Nyiregyhaza? Can you tell us about it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I only remember a small part of the town. I was eight years old when I left the town. So my memory's of immediate trips from my home to my grandmother's house. She also had a store in the middle of town. She-- she sold paint and groceries, and it was an interesting trip as a child, to come from my house to that store to get candies and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to school. The school-- we-- we didn't have any automobiles, that I can recall. When my father took a train to Budapest, we would see him to the station, and it was in a horse and a buggy. Not our horse and a buggy, that was the taxi in the town. We would go into the middle of the town, and the horse and buggy would take us to the train station. I was-- I never left Nyiregyhaza up until 1994 when I was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never played with too many of the children in town, only the Jewish children. That's what we were. It was-- it was a very close, Jewish knit community. That's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a Jewish section of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't-- I can't say that there was a section, but it was a very small area that the Jews lived. Our neighbors were not Jewish. But we didn't have any contact with the non-Jewish people in the community. They didn't-- they didn't come into our home, and we didn't go into their home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your dad went to Budapest. What kind of work did Dad do? Tell us about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman. He traveled to Vienna, Budapest. He was a very-- I have fond memories of him as a very elegant, intelligent man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was slim. And we had-- I don't know if you remember the-- the stoves that, in Europe, have the-- with ceramics. I remember him standing, always, by it, warming his hands. And the last memory of my father is when-- before they-- before we were going to leave this town. He had a little drink with me, a little sweet liqueur, and I felt very, very grown at the time. And he told me that he will never come back, and that I should take good care of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very beautiful-- younger, much younger, than my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She-- she was very angry at him when we left. Again, you're asking me to remember prior to 1944. I have a problem with that. I can't. It seems that whenever-- that's what I remember-- that year. It's like my whole life began then. My mother didn't want me to go to this Christian family that my father arranged for me to go to in case we were deported. There were rumors that we were going to be deported. And nobody believed it because they believed that the Hungarian government would never allow this, although all the signs were there. The Polish children were coming across. We were hiding them. Some Czechoslovakian children-- even in my own home we were hiding. But everyone said, it can happen in any other country, but never in Hungary. I had a very famous lawyer uncle who said, it can never happen. The Hungarian government won't let it. My father didn't believe it. My father knew all along that it will happen, and he took precaution. He-- he arranged for me to be hidden, and he arranged for my mother and himself to be hidden in a monastery as a nun and as a priest. What was your father's first name? Laszlo, L-A-S-Z-L-O. Laszlo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lilly, L-I-L-L-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when dad was born or about how old he was in 1944?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was 44. And mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was 13 years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: The famous uncle who you mentioned who was a lawyer, what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bohm. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jenu. Jenu Bohm, B-O-H-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that you did go to school. Do you have any memories of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prior to '44? Two incidents-- learning Hebrew, to write, read in Hebrew and having very great difficulties, and playing with some of the children in the schoolyard. I don't remember a classroom. I remember almost nothing. Well, it was-- I guess, it was second or third grade-- second grade-- that I was in elementary school. But I do not remember at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any incidents of anti-Semitism that occurred before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before '44? No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your family arrive in the town of Nyiregyhaza? Had they been there for generations or had they come recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my grandmother lived there for-- I would say, in the 1800s. That's when they moved to Nyiregyhaza-- my grandmother and my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother's name, yes. Reggie. Reggie Schlesenger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was she in 1944-- the time you remember, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Jenu. Jenu, and he passed away prior to the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the trip from your house to your grandmother's store was a very interesting one . Do you recall the reason why-- what the details were about that? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. From your childhood, from that time, do you remember any special holidays or any special family get-togethers that might have remained in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we used to go to my grandmother's for Passover. That I remember very well. Lots of children. My grandmother had six children. And we all went to her Passover.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Sunday mornings, the housekeeper would take me over there to visit. And all the six children were in one room, and I was the baby and everybody was playing with me. See? I do remember. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I, also, remember my Uncle Micky leaving for America. That was in 1939. What was Micky's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Micky Bohm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Micky Bohm. Why did he decide to leave for America in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things were getting bad and everybody chipped in-- all the children and the husbands like my father. They all chipped in and sent him to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: He survived in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived in America, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to meet him afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the one that sent us our affidavit and brought everybody to America eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of those six children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. Of course, Micky, Bela. Bela survived. Elizabeth, Edith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Elizabeth survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth survived. Edith survived. Lilly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are they today? Where's Elizabeth today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- Elizabeth lives in Hewlett Harbor who was also interviewed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Edith Stern lives in Forest Hill. She was also interviewed. Micky is no longer with us. He passed away a few years ago. And his last name was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Also Bohm. Bela stayed in Hungary after the war, got married, and he-- he drowned in the Danube.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, what was her last name? Bela's last name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Bela was, also, Bohm. This was all six children.

speaker: interviewee
text: All Bohms. Yes. And there was another uncle, Immy, who came back after the war, found me where I was hiding when no one else was back. And he went up to Budapest looking for my parents and died of typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1945. You mentioned that most of your memory begins in 1944. Was there a single event that, for you, signaled the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in, and I remember all the adults were very nervous and worried. And we were told that in order to leave our home, we had to wear a yellow star, and it was very scary for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans came in on March 19, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Correct? But prior to that, there were anti-Jewish laws that were passed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall-- since 1938, do you recall any of these things happening around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my father being taken to a labor camp and my mother trying to have a doctor help him because he had ulcers, very bad ulcers, and we were worried about his health. I remember he had a very lucrative business that was taken away from him and given to some Gentile people to run. We were told that it-- it will be given back to him, but that wasn't the case. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that business taken away from him? Did he have a license?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a-- it was a-- he had the first factory where they were making canned goods. It was quite unusual those days for Hungary. And he tried to make it survive by taking in partners that were not Jewish, but one day, they just came in, and they said, take your coat and leave. That's how it was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these people, they were Hungarians. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. They were Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before the Nazis came?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was before the Nazis came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year would that have been? 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that probably would be in about '41, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1941. OK. You mentioned the possibility that Dad would be taken away for labor. When did the community start to hear about people being taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was whispers. There was no announcements or nobody that we knew. And even the Jews had-- were afraid to talk among themselves. So one day, this one-- we-- we heard that might have been taken to labor camp and, next, somebody else. But my father was taken away. He, actually, was taken away from Nyiregyhaza, but my mother got this doctor's letter and went to see him and got him released to come back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that. Tell us how Dad was taken away, but then, you were able to get him back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was, again, sometimes, we wondered, in retrospect, that he would have been better off-- had he been in a labor camp, he might have survived. We-- he-- he-- we thought we saved him by bringing him back home because he really was a very sick man-- a young man and a very sick man-- stomach, always had stomach problems. That was, finally, at the end-- that's how he died in concentration camp because of the bad stomach. My mother had connections with a doctor, took a letter. I guess, some financial arrangements were made, and my father was allowed to come back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Dad chosen to go to the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew that he was being taken away to a labor camp, and once he left the house, then your mother was able to arrange this special letter from a doctor and was successful in returning him to home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German Occupation that began in March of 1944-- you mentioned the law of the yellow star. Can you tell us what happened-- that yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: The yellow star was the beginning. It was the least of the problem. About two, three weeks later, they showed a certain part of the town which became the, so-called, ghetto. Our home became part of the ghetto. We were right in the center of the ghetto. We had a beautiful home-- beautiful large home. And if I remember correctly, my mother mentioned to me about 17 families were brought to live in this one home. One of my relatives was-- had a problem being with people. It was a-- there's a-- there's a illness where you can't be social. Where she was kind of-- she liked-- she wanted to be alone, and she lived in our pantry. We were given a bedroom. My mother, my father, and I lived in a bedroom. My room was given to a family. The living room was given to two families. The bed-- I mean, even the kitchen, families were living. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next door to us, we owned the house, also. In-- you know, in Hungary, they have courtyards. And in these courtyards, we had our home and another home. We owned both. We had a tenant. And this tenant, originally-- she was Hungarian, but she had some connections to German-- German background. So she was-- she and her husband were entertaining German soldiers while we were in this ghetto. That, I remember-- how before this happened, she used to be nice to me-- the lady. After that, she never spoke to me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the Germans came in in March of '44. Before the Germans came in, there were Jews that were brought to the town from Poland and children came from Slovakia. Did you have any contact with these Jews who had been brought in, all these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Just one child who lived in my home. The Jewish community was taking care of these people, and they asked-- they gave us one child to take care of until the parents can be found. And-- and she was with us until 1944, and then she was deported with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this child tell you-- do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva. And last name? About how old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she tell you what happened in her town and how her Jewish community had been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was telling us that they were beaten. And one-- one night her mother and father just put her onto the street, and said, go across the-- it was like a little creek to get into Hungary from Czechoslovakia. And she just walked across the creek and on her own-- a child-- and until she found the first Jewish person that she could. And that's how she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she told this story to your family, did your family imagine that this could possibly happen to your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father could. My father always believed that this can happen. No one else did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this what sparked the interest in your father to make plans for you and your mother in case of a deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he start to make those plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as the Germans walked into Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: March of '44. Can you tell us what you know of those plans that your father had arranged?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all fell apart. My mother refused to leave her mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Refused, said I won't leave. My father begged. They gave me to a Christian family to hide me. Three days later, my mother carried on and made such a scene that they had to bring me back. And you can imagine what it was to smuggle me out of the ghetto and then, smuggle me back into the ghetto. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father and mother fought through the whole time about this. My father, finally, at the very end, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] refused to listen and on the-- we were on the wagons to-- see, the-- the way they deported us from Nyiregyhaza, they took everyone to a small little town called Nyirbator, where there were the trains to go to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us on these wagons with-- horses were taking big wagons. We took all our belongings and suitcases because we were going to have a-- we-- we-- nobody knew where we were going. We were going to labor camps, that's what they said. And we were-- I was on this wagon going to the train, and we were going to-- we were going through town and all the townspeople were all lined up, looking at us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was sitting on top of this mountain of-- of luggage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the men and the women were walking. Only the children and the old people were allowed to be on the-- on the wagon. And I was wondering why all these people were looking at us. I knew them all. And they were looking, and I was wondering why they're not helping my mother to-- and then, my father sent word to this same gentleman who he knew, who he worked under in the-- in that-- in that very same labor camp that I was telling you about-- and found out that this man needed money very badly and wanted money very badly. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father sent my aunt, the one that is in Hewlett Harbor now, with a huge bag of money. My aunt took me off the wagon, took me to the temple of town. I don't know how the two of us ever got through the stamp or how we were able to move around, but we did, and we got there. And I waited there for an hour. And then, I was told to go outside to meet this man. I already knew him because this is the same man I lived with for three days prior. And I went out, and I had to walk into town, take off my star, and just walk through town and meet him. The trouble is, I forgot to take off my star.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was walking in this-- with this big luggage full of money into town, eight years old with my yellow star. And somebody started pointing at-- pointing to me. Look at that. That's Marika. And so then, I remembered. I took the star, and I remember throwing it on the floor. And I was told to walk into this huge movie theater in town. And he was there waiting for me. And that's how I got away from the deportation. Your father had made these plans much earlier you had mentioned, back in March of 1944. The story that you just told us took place at the deportations, which would be late May or early June. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Late May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Late May is when you were deported. But your father made plans in March for you to be hidden along with your mother and you had mentioned in a monastery. Might you know some details about how dad was able to arrange something like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, while the people that took me did it for monetary reasons, the monastery, the nuns and the priest outside of-- of Nyiregyhaza, were going to-- they just wanted to help my father. They knew him prior. They liked him. And they were willing to hide him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the motivation for the nuns and the priest-- they were Roman-Catholic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was a humanitarian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Humanitarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motivation. They were willing to hide you. Was-- was there risk involved for the nuns and the priests to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huge. They would have been killed, obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you would have been found? You were successfully brought to the monastery?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. My mother and father were supposed to go to the monastery. I was going to this Christian family where I was put in a bed and hidden for two years-- in a room in a bed. Excuse me, two years. From 1944 to 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: So back in March of '44, your mom and your dad were supposed to go to the monastery?

speaker: interviewee
text: They never went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they never went because Mom did not want to leave her mother. And you were supposed to go to a private family--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were going to hide you. And they succeeded in bringing you to the private family. Tell us about that private family that you went to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a grandmother. There was a mother. There were two children. The grandmother was very nice to me. The rest of the family was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their motivation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was for the money. I am grateful to them. They saved my life, but they did not treat me well. And I'd rather-- that part-- that part of my life, I'd like not to talk about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll stop the tape now, and we'll continue with this point on the next tape.
